perturbation.original_text: Any notices or delivery required here will be deemed completed when hand-delivered, delivered by agent, or seven (7) days after being placed in the post, postage prepaid, to the Members at the addresses contained in this Agreement or as the Members may later designate in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: Any notices or delivery required here will be deemed completed when emailed, or one (1) day after being placed in the post, postage prepaid, to the Members at the addresses contained in this Agreement or as the Members may later designate in writing.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period to one day may not provide sufficient time for the other party to respond or take necessary actions, especially if the notice involves critical deadlines or legal implications. Furthermore, emails are not always a legally sound method of delivery of notice.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 671.202 - Notice; Knowledge; Notice of Facts; U.C.C. - Article 1 - General Provisions
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statutes § 671.202 outlines the requirements for providing sufficient notice. Reducing the notification period to one day after mailing may not allow reasonable time to take action, and accepting email as the only valid form of contact when it may not be read for a long time can violate business standards. What constitutes sufficient notice depends on the nature, purpose, and circumstances of the action, making a one-day period potentially insufficient and therefore contradictory.

Home > Laws > 2022 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 671 > Section 202 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2022 Florida Statutes (Including 2022C, 2022D, 2022A, and 2023B) Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 671 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE: GENERAL PROVISIONS Entire Chapter SECTION 202 Prima facie evidence by third-party documents. F.S. 671.202 671.202 Prima facie evidence by third-party documents. — A document in due form purporting to be a bill of lading, policy or certificate of insurance, official weigher’s or inspector’s certificate, consular invoice, or any other document authorized or required by the contract to be issued by a third party is prima facie evidence of its own authenticity and genuineness and of the facts stated in the document by the third party. History. — s. 1, ch. 65-254; s. 9, ch. 2007-134. Note. — s. 1-202, U.C.C.
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the notification period from seven days to one day and introduces email as a valid method of delivery. The Law Explanation states that reducing the notification period to one day after mailing may not allow reasonable time to take action, potentially contradicting sufficient notice requirements. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't directly specify the time or method of notification that would be deemed sufficient, it points to Florida Statute § 671.202, which is relevant to determining sufficient notice. Therefore, by shortening the timeframe and introducing email, the modified text creates a potential contradiction because the one-day period may not provide enough time for the other party to act, particularly considering the circumstances, and email may not be a legally sound delivery method.

perturbation.original_text: The Members submit to the jurisdiction of the courts of the State of Florida for the enforcement of this Agreement and for any arbitration award or decision arising from this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Members submit to the jurisdiction of the courts of Broward County for the enforcement of this Agreement and for any arbitration award or decision arising from this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: While parties can agree on venue, limiting jurisdiction solely to Broward County may conflict with Florida law regarding venue requirements, particularly if the cause of action arises outside of Broward County or involves parties not located there.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 47.011 - Where actions may be begun
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 47.011 dictates where actions may be begun, specifying that actions can be brought where the cause of action accrued, where the property in question is located, or where the defendant resides. Limiting jurisdiction solely to Broward County, regardless of these other factors, directly contradicts this statute. A cause of action or dispute under this agreement may arise outside of Broward County, so limiting jurisdiction to only Broward County would go against the statute.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title VI > Chapter 47 > Section 011 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title VI CIVIL PRACTICE AND PROCEDURE Chapter 47 VENUE Entire Chapter SECTION 011 Where actions may be begun. F.S. 47.011 47.011 Where actions may be begun. — Actions shall be brought only in the county where the defendant resides, where the cause of action accrued, or where the property in litigation is located. This section shall not apply to actions against nonresidents. History. — s. 7, Nov. 21, 1829; s. 1, ch. 3721, 1887; RS 998; GS 1383; RGS 2579; CGL 4219; s. 24, ch. 57-1; s. 12, ch. 63-572; s. 6, ch. 65-1; s. 3, ch. 67-254; s. 11, ch. 73-334. Note. — Former s. 46.01.
perturbation.justification: The modified text limits jurisdiction to Broward County, which contradicts Florida Statute 47.011. The original text allowed jurisdiction in the courts of the State of Florida. The changed text limits the venue. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that actions shall be brought in the county where the defendant resides, where the cause of action accrued, or where the property in litigation is located. By limiting jurisdiction only to Broward County, the modified text prevents actions from being brought in other counties where the cause of action accrued or where the defendant resides, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This section will survive for one year after the expiration or termination of this Agreement or dissolution of the Venture.
perturbation.changed_text: This section will survive for six months after the expiration or termination of this Agreement or dissolution of the Venture.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality survival period to six months may be insufficient to protect trade secrets or other confidential information under Florida law, especially considering the nature of IT development and medical services involved.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 688.002 - Uniform Trade Secrets Act
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (FUTSA) provides legal protection for trade secrets. While FUTSA does not explicitly define a specific time frame for confidentiality, reducing the protection period to six months may be deemed unreasonable in cases involving long-term trade secrets, thus conflicting with the intended protection afforded by FUTSA. The six-month window may not adequately protect trade secrets associated with IT development and medical services, which usually requires a longer period of confidentiality.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 688 > Section 002 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 688 UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT Entire Chapter SECTION 002 Definitions. F.S. 688.002 688.002 Definitions. — As used in ss. 688.001 - 688.009 , unless the context requires otherwise: (1) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) “Misappropriation” means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or 2. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that her or his knowledge of the trade secret was: a. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; b. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or c. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Before a material change of her or his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) “Person” means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) “Trade secret” means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process that: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (b) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. History. — s. 2, ch. 88-254; s. 750, ch. 97-102.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the survival period for confidentiality from one year to six months, which the Law Explanation states may be deemed unreasonable under Florida's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (FUTSA) for long-term trade secrets, particularly in IT development and medical services. Snippet 1 defines a "trade secret" as information that derives economic value from not being generally known and is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy. Reducing the confidentiality period to six months could be argued as not a 'reasonable' effort to maintain secrecy, thus contradicting the intent of FUTSA to protect trade secrets.
